elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce Freqformer, a novel Transformer-based architecture designed for 3-D, high-definition visualization of human retinal circulation from a single scan in commercial optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA).
elements.sent-2411-002: Freqformer addresses the challenge of limited signal-to-noise ratio in OCTA volume by utilizing a complex-valued frequency-domain module (CFDM) and a simplified multi-head attention (Sim-MHA) mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-003: Using merged volumes as ground truth, Freqformer enables accurate reconstruction of retinal vasculature across the depth planes, allowing for 3-D quantification of capillary segments (count, density, and length).
elements.sent-2411-004: Our method outperforms state-of-the-art convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and several Transformer-based models, with superior performance in peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR), structural similarity index measure (SSIM), and learned perceptual image patch similarity (LPIPS).
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, Freqformer demonstrates excellent generalizability across lower scanning density, effectively enhancing OCTA scans with larger fields of view (from 3×3 mm² to 6×6 mm² and 12×12 mm²).
elements.sent-2411-006: These results suggest that Freqformer can significantly improve the understanding and characterization of retinal circulation, offering potential clinical applications in diagnosing and managing retinal vascular diseases.
elements.sent-2411-007: Recently, as a functional extension of optical coherence tomography (OCT), OCT angiography (OCTA) is emerging to resolve retinal microvasculature in vivo by detecting the moving contrast of blood flow~\cite{jia2012split .}
elements.sent-2411-008: Although OCTA can provide 3-D visualization of retinal circulation, current analysis primarily relies on 2-D OCTA en face images projected from specific slabs.
elements.sent-2411-009: The en face approach may oversimplify the inherently 3-D nature of the OCTA volume, limiting its potential to fully elucidate the complex architecture of the retinal microvascular network, constraining the inherent 3-D nature of OCTA volume and diminishing its significance in understanding 3-D retinal microvascular network.
elements.sent-2411-010: A critical technical challenge in performing direct 3-D analysis of OCTA volumes arises from their limited quality, characterized by a low signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and poor vascular connectivity across individual depth planes.
elements.sent-2411-011: As a result, the retinal circulation remains incompletely understood, particularly in terms of its 3-D organization and intricate relationship with the retinal layers ~\cite{chan2015vivo,ishibazawa2019retinal .}

elements.sent-2411-012: Shape-based filtering (such as Hessian filtering~\cite{meleppat2019multiscale ), has been widely used to enhance OCTA volume.}
elements.sent-2411-013: It works by identifying and enhancing the tubular structures corresponding to blood vessels.
elements.sent-2411-014: However, it is highly sensitive to noise due to the second-order derivatives~\cite{truc2009vessel and frequently causes ghost blood vessels.}
elements.sent-2411-015: Other conventional algorithms, such as wavelet transform~\cite{liu2020using and compressive sensing~\cite{wang2022compressive , also face challenges in achieving an optimal balance between noise reduction and vessel enhancement.}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Recently, deep learning has emerged as a promising approach for enhancing retinal circulations~\cite{tan2023oct2former .}
elements.sent-2411-017: These methods can be broadly categorized into two avenues: 1) Super-resolution-based approach.
elements.sent-2411-018: In this method, networks are trained using paired low-resolution (such as 6\(\times\)6 \(\text m \text m^{\text 2}\)) and high-resolution (3\(\times\)3 \(\text m \text m^{\text 2}\)) angiograms to enhance the OCTA images~\cite{gao2020reconstruction,yuan2022image .. 2) Frame-averaging-based approach.}
elements.sent-2411-019: Here, the network takes a single scan image as input and uses a merged image as ground truth to enhance the angiograms~\cite{kadomoto2020enhanced .}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, these methods are limited to 2-D en face projection images due to the low quality of individual depth plane images in OCTA, making the training process highly challenging.
elements.sent-2411-021: To the best of our knowledge, the only reported approach for obtaining high-definition 3-D vasculature from a single OCTA scan involves training a network to segment blood vessels~\cite{stefan2020deep,li2024visualization , which is a simplified task for enhancing the angiograms.}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, the manual annotation of numerous depth plane images required by this method is highly time-consuming and poses significant challenges for annotators in accurately labeling capillaries.
elements.sent-2411-023: Moreover, the lack of angiogram de-correlation information in segmentation limits its utility \cite{su2016calibration .}
elements.sent-2411-024: In this work, we introduce Freqformer, a frequency-domain Transformer-based architecture designed to reconstruct the 3-D organization of retinal circulation from a single commercial OCTA scan.
elements.sent-2411-025: It takes advantage of Transformer~\cite{vaswani2017attention,yao2024pscat,tan2023oct2former,li2024structurally , a revolutionary technique with self-attention mechanism to mitigate the limitation of CNNs (small receptive field struggling to capture global (long-range pixel) interactions).}
elements.sent-2411-026: The highlights of Freqformer include: (1) Complex-Valued Frequency-Domain Module (CFDM) which leverages frequency information to address Transformers' limitations in extracting high-frequency features, significantly enhancing the resolution and connectivity of OCTA images.
elements.sent-2411-027: The study was conducted following strict ethical guidelines, with all participants signing informed consent forms, in compliance with the Declaration of Helsinki.
elements.sent-2411-028: Ethical approval was obtained from the institutional review boards of the University of Pittsburgh.
elements.sent-2411-029: OCT/A scans were acquired using a PLEX® Elite 9000 swept-source OCT (ZEISS, Dublin, CA).
elements.sent-2411-030: Each imaging session consisted of 400 A-lines per B-scan, and 400 B-scan per volume, with a total of 10 repeated volumes, covering a 3\(\times\)3 \(\text{mm ^{\text 2 \) field of view across various retinal regions.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: To generate ground truth, the 10 repeated volumes of each OCT/A session were registered and merged using recently developed algorithms~\cite{wang2023volumetrically,pi2020imaging .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Briefly, the 3-D registration algorithm independently aligned the lateral and axial directions, taking advantage of the negligible angle variation of the probing light with the retinal tissue, resulting in nearly invariant A-line profiles across repeats.
elements.sent-2411-033: In the lateral direction, non-rigid registration was performed by aligning retinal vascular patterns in en face images.
elements.sent-2411-034: In the axial direction, a depth transformation matrix was computed by calculating the cross-correlation of A-line profiles and identifying the axial shifts with maximal correlation values.
elements.sent-2411-035: The registered volumes were then averaged to produce high-definition OCT/A volumes as the ground truth for training.

elements.sent-2411-036: To accurately capture true retinal circulation in 3-D, removing tail artifacts of blood vessels is a critical step~\cite{bernucci2018investigation .}
elements.sent-2411-037: Several algorithms have been developed to address this issue~\cite{zhang2016projection, liu2019projection, li2022blood .}
elements.sent-2411-038: However, they were developed to maximize integrity of capillary plexuses in en face images, often neglecting the actual caliber of retinal vessels.
elements.sent-2411-039: As a result, they tend to lose penetrating and connecting vessels/capillaries, limiting their utility for appreciating 3-D retinal circulation.
elements.sent-2411-040: To address this challenge, we propose a moving average filter (MAF) method.
elements.sent-2411-041: This method leverages the fact that artifact voxels typically have smaller angiogram values than the real vessel voxels located anteriorly.
elements.sent-2411-042: Specifically, to obtain an artifact-free OCTA volume \(A_{\text PR}\), we designed a moving filter with a size of 50 \(\upmu\)m along the axial direction with an empirical residual coefficient of 0.8 (Eq. 1).
elements.sent-2411-043: As demonstrated in Fig. 1, the proposed method effectively removed tail artifacts from the superficial capillary plexus (SCP), preserving the integrity of the intermediate capillary plexus (ICP) and deep capillary plexus (DCP).
elements.sent-2411-044: The caliber of retinal microvasculature in the post-removal OCTA B-scan images resembled that observed in cross-sectional OCT B-scan images, ensuring excellent preservation of penetrating vessels and capillaries.
elements.sent-2411-045: These results underscore the effectiveness of the proposed MAF method for robust and accurate tail artifact removal in 3-D OCTA imaging. \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} A_{\text PR} = \sqrt{A_{n}^{2} - 0.8 \sum_{i=n-11}^{n-1} A_{i}^{2}} \end{aligned} \label{eq}\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-046: Given an input OCTA image \(\mathbf{I \in \mathbb{R ^{H\times W\times 1 \), Freqformer first applies a 3\(\times\)3 convolutional layer to extract shallow feature \(\mathbf{F_{0 \in \mathbb{R ^{H\times W\times C \).}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: After that, the encoder-decoder structure was used to extract the deep features with encoders reducing the spatial resolution of the feature map while doubling the number of channels using pixel-unshuffle operation as the stage grows, and decoders performing the opposite operations to restore the original resolution.
elements.sent-2411-048: At stages 1-3, up-sampled feature maps are concatenated with their corresponding encoder output, with channel dimension reduced by half using a 1\(\times\)1 convolutional layer.
elements.sent-2411-049: These skip connections preserve crucial spatial information and minimize the loss of fine details during the down-sampling and up-sampling processes.
elements.sent-2411-050: Additionally, to improve generalization, a drop path rate of 0.1 is applied, randomly dropping paths during training to regularize the network.
elements.sent-2411-051: After passing through the encoder-decoder, feature maps undergo further refinement, resulting in \(\mathbf{I_{r \in \mathbb{R ^{H\times W\times 1 \) after a 3\(\times\)3 convolutional layer.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-052: The enhanced OCTA image is then generated by adding \(\mathbf{I}\) to \(\mathbf{I_{r }\), effectively merging the original image with the refined deep features to produce a high-quality output.}
elements.sent-2411-053: Each stage of the encoder-decoder architecture and refinement comprises multiple Freqformer blocks.
elements.sent-2411-054: In each block, the feature map \(\mathbf{F_{i \in \mathbb{R ^{H\times W\times C \) is split into two parts \(\mathbf{X_{1 \in \mathbb{R ^{H\times W\times \alpha C \) and \(\mathbf{X_{2 \in \mathbb{R ^{H\times W\times (1 - \alpha) C \) along the channel dimension.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: This dual-branch strategy enables Freqformer to leverage complementary spatial and frequency domain features, ensuring enhanced representation and reconstruction capabilities for OCTA images.
elements.sent-2411-056: However, MHA's quadratic space complexity \(\mathcal{O (hH^{2 W^{2 )\) pose challenges for OCTA images with large size as it significantly exceeds the linear complexity of CNNs \(\mathcal{O (HWC_{out )\).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: To address this, we introduce cross-covariance (CC) attention~\cite{ali2021xcit , where attention weights \(\mathbf{A \in \mathbb{R ^{h\times C\times C \) are computed across channels instead of tokens, using the cross-covariance matrix \(\mathbf{\hat{K ^{T \mathbf{\hat{Q }\).}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: This approach reduces space complexity to \(\mathcal{O (C^{2 / h)\), making it scale linearly with image size.}}
elements.sent-2411-059: Specifically, in standard MHA, there are four linear transformations (\(W_{q}\), \(W_{k}\), \(W_{v}\) , and \(W_{projection}\)) applied to compute queries (\(\mathbf{Q}\)), keys (\(\mathbf{K}\)), values(\(\mathbf{V}\)), and output features.
elements.sent-2411-060: However, \(W_{v}\) and \(W_{projection}\) are removed because their identity components tend to become dominant as Transformer converges~\cite{trockman2023mimetic, he2023simplifying .}
elements.sent-2411-061: To improve local context representation, a 3\(\times\)3 depth-wise convolutional layer (DConv) is integrated after the \(W_{q}\) and \(W_{k}\) linear transformations, implemented via 1\(\times\)1 convolution.
elements.sent-2411-062: Unlike conventional vanilla convolutional layers, DConv operates channel-wise, rather than across all channels, thereby reducing computational cost to \(\sim1/2C\).
elements.sent-2411-063: The Sim-MHA operation is defined as: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \mathbf{X}_{\text{Sim-MHA}}= \mathbf{\hat{X}} \cdot \mathrm{softmax} (\mathbf{\hat{K}}^{T} \mathbf{\hat{Q}}/\tau), \mathbf{K} = \mathrm{LN}(\mathbf{X})W_{k} W_{D} , \mathbf{Q} = \mathrm{LN}(\mathbf{X})W_{q} W_{D}, \end{aligned} \label{eq1}\end{equation} where \(W_{D}\) and \(\mathrm{LN (\cdot) \) represent the depth-wise convolution transformation layer and the layer normalization, respectively.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation} X_{Sim-MHA}= \mathbf{\hat{X}} \cdot softmax (\mathbf{\hat{K}}^{T} \mathbf{\hat{Q}}/\tau), K = LN(X)W_{k} W_{D} , Q = LN(X)W_{q} W_{D},  \label{eq1}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-064: In the proposed DFFN, two linear transformations are applied using 1\(\times\)1 convolutions to expand and reduce channels.
elements.sent-2411-065: A 3\(\times\)3 DConv layer, similar to that in Sim-MHA, is integrated to enhance local context representation.
elements.sent-2411-066: The skip connection used in conventional MHA is removed, directly connecting the input to the DFFN output.
elements.sent-2411-067: DFFN is defined as: \begin{equation}\label{eq2} \text{ReLU}((\mathrm{LN} (\mathbf{X}_{\text{Sim-MHA}})W_{p}^{0}+b_{p}^{0})W_{D}+b_{D})W_{p}^{1}+b_{p}^{1}+\mathbf{X},\end{equation} where \(b_{p ^{0}\), \(b_{p ^{1}\), and \(b_{D}\) are biases of linear transformations \(W_{p ^{0}\) and \(W_{p ^{1}\), and the 3\(\times\)3 DConv layer \(W_{D}\), respectively.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: This summation process, whether simplified or not, tends to diminish high-frequency features in the Transformer~\cite{park2022vision .}
elements.sent-2411-069: To address this, we introduce CFDM, which directly learns complex features in the frequency domain~\cite{li2023frequency .}

elements.sent-2411-070: In CFDM, a real-valued fast Fourier transform (RFFT) is applied to the feature map \(\mathbf{X \in \mathbb{R ^{H\times W\times C \), generating a frequency representation \(\mathcal{F (\mathbf{X ) \in \mathbb{C ^{H\times [\frac{W {2 +1] \times C \), reducing redundancy and computational cost without losing information due to Hermitian symmetry.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-071: After the RFFT, two complex-valued convolutional layers (CConv) are employed to process frequency-domain features~\cite{wang2023axial .}
elements.sent-2411-072: For an input \(\mathcal{F (\mathbf{X )=x+iy\) and a convolution \(\mathbf{W =u+iv\), the operation is \(\mathcal{F (\mathbf{X )*\mathbf{W =(x*u-y*v)+i(x*v+y*u)\).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-073: Therefore, the gradient of back-propagation can be expressed as: \begin{equation}\frac{\partial L(\mathcal{F}(\mathbf{X})*\mathbf{W})}{\partial \mathbf{W}} = \frac{\partial L(\mathcal{F}(\mathbf{X})*\mathbf{W})}{\partial \mathcal{F}(\mathbf{X})*\mathbf{W}}*\mathcal{F}(\mathbf{X}), \label{eq3}\end{equation} where \(L(\cdot)\) denotes the loss function.
elements.sent-2411-074: The real part of \(\frac{\partial L(\mathcal{F (\mathbf{X )*\mathbf{W ) {\partial \mathbf{W }\) is \(\mathfrak{R}\left(\frac{\partial L(\mathcal{F (\mathbf{X )*\mathbf{W ) {\partial \mathcal{F (\mathbf{X )*\mathbf{W }\right) * x-\mathfrak{I}\left(\frac{\partial L(\mathcal{F (\mathbf{X )*\mathbf{W ) {\partial \mathcal{F (\mathbf{X )*\mathbf{W }\right) * y\) while the imaginary part is \(\mathfrak{R}\left(\frac{\partial L(\mathcal{F (\mathbf{X )*\mathbf{W ) {\partial \mathcal{F (\mathbf{X )*\mathbf{W }\right) * y+\mathfrak{I}\left(\frac{\partial L(\mathcal{F (\mathbf{X )*\mathbf{W ) {\partial \mathcal{F (\mathbf{X )*\mathbf{W }\right) * x\).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: It is worth to noting that, although the receptive field of CConv is limited, it still captures global context as each frequency component represents a global characteristic of the entire image.
elements.sent-2411-076: Next, CReLU, which performs conventional ReLU on the real part and the imaginary part of feature maps separately, conducts non-linear activation~\cite{trabelsi2017deep .}
elements.sent-2411-077: However, this activation approach can alter the amplitude and phase simultaneously, potentially disrupting the original frequency information.
elements.sent-2411-078: To mitigate this, we introduce a dynamic skip connection between the original complex-valued matrix and the output in the frequency domain, controlled by a trainable parameter \(\beta\).
elements.sent-2411-079: Finally, inverse RFFT is employed to convert the frequency-domain enhanced feature back to spatial domain.
elements.sent-2411-080: To quantify retinal circulation, enhanced OCTA volumes are binarized using a thresholding approach, where voxels with angiogram values larger than the threshold (50/255) are determined as blood vessels.
elements.sent-2411-081: As described in our previous work~\cite{pi2020retinal , the 3-D binary masks are then skeletonized to shrink vascular diameter and highlight connectivity information.}
elements.sent-2411-082: Next, the number of neighboring vascular voxels is counted for each voxel in 3-D space to identify bifurcation points, which are characterized by having exactly three neighboring vascular voxels.
elements.sent-2411-083: These bifurcation points are unique among body points of blood vessels with two neighboring vascular voxels and end points with only one neighboring vascular voxel.
elements.sent-2411-084: Therefore, by removing these bifurcation points, vessel segments can be isolated and extracted automatically.
elements.sent-2411-085: It should be noted that small fragments with lengths equal or less than six voxels are considered as noise and excluded from the analysis.
elements.sent-2411-086: Quantitative metrics, including capillary segment count (\(N\)), segment density (\(N/mm^{3}\)), segment length (voxel), are calculated all within the inner retina volume.
elements.sent-2411-087: Statistical comparisons among the three groups (single scan, enhanced scan, and merged scan) are performed using one-way ANOVA followed by Tukey's HSD test.
elements.sent-2411-088: A p-value smaller than 0.05 is considered significant in this study.
elements.sent-2411-089: We randomly selected N=3,600 single-merged pairs of depth plane OCTA images (400 px \(\times\) 400 px) from 20 scans across 14 subjects.
elements.sent-2411-090: The dataset was divided into 90\% for training and 10\% for validation.
elements.sent-2411-091: An additional 400 pairs of images from extra scans not included in the training data, were used to evaluate performance.
elements.sent-2411-092: The number of Freqformer blocks was set to [2, 4, 4, 6, 4, 4, 2] in the stage of encoder-decoder, and to 4 in the refinement module.
elements.sent-2411-093: Following typical settings in other Transformer methods\cite{zamir2022restormer,zhang2023accurate , the number of heads in each stage of the encoder-decoder was set to [1, 2, 4, 8, 4, 2, 1], with 1 head used in the refinement module.}
elements.sent-2411-094: Additionally, the number of channels was 48 for the feature map input into the encoder-decoder.
elements.sent-2411-095: In the Transformer layer, the Sim-MHA employed bias-free linear transformations, while the DFFN used biased linear transformations, with an expansion factor of 2.66.
elements.sent-2411-096: The model was trained using AdamW optimizer~\cite{loshchilov2018decoupled (\(\beta_{1}\) = 0.9, \(\beta_{2}\) = 0.999, weight decay of \(1e^{-4}\)) and \(L_{1}\) loss.}
elements.sent-2411-097: The batch size was set to 1, and the model was trained for 480,000 iterations with an initial learning rate \(1e^{-4}\), halved every 20 epochs until convergence.
elements.sent-2411-098: The reference networks were trained following their original implementation details.
elements.sent-2411-099: All experiments were implemented in Python 3.8 with Pytorch 2.2.0 deep learning framework on a single NVIDIA A100 GPU.
elements.sent-2411-100: The performance of Freqformer was assessed using three image quality metrics: peak signal-to-noise ratio (PSNR), structural similarity index measure (SSIM), and learned perceptual image patch similarity (LPIPS), as detailed below: \begin{equation}\text { PSNR }=10 \log _{10}\left(\frac{\max (I)^{2}}{\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left(I(i)-I_{\mathrm{GT}}(i)\right)^{2}}\right), \label{eq4}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\text { SSIM }=\frac{\left(2 \mu_{I} \mu_{\mathrm{I_{GT}}}+C_{1}\right)\left(2 \sigma_{II_{\mathrm{GT}}}+C_{2}\right)}{\left(\mu_{I}^{2}+\mu_{I_{\mathrm{GT}}}^{2}+C_{1}\right)\left(\sigma_{I}^{2}+\sigma_{I_{\mathrm{GT}}}^{2}+C_{2}\right)} , \label{eq5}\end{equation} \begin{equation}\text{LPIPS} = \sum_{l} \omega_{l} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N}\left\| \phi_l (I(i)) - \phi_l (I_\mathrm{GT}(i)) \right\|_2^2 , \label{eq6}\end{equation} where \(I\) and \(I_\mathrm{GT}\) are the enhanced image and the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-101: N is the number of pixels in the image.
elements.sent-2411-102: The constants \(C_{1}\) and \(C_{2}\) in SSIM are set to 0.01 and 0.03.
elements.sent-2411-103: These metrics were used to comprehensively evaluate image reconstruction quality, focusing on fidelity and perceptual similarity to the ground truth.

elements.subsection-2411-006: Performance and Comparison with Other Methods
elements.sent-2411-104: To evaluate the effectiveness of the proposed Freqformer, we compared it with several state-of-the-art networks, including three transformer-based models and four CNN-based models.
elements.sent-2411-105: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig3 , the capillaries in the depth plane image from single scan (input) exhibit discontinuity and noise, making it difficult to study the intricate retinal circulation.}
elements.sent-2411-106: Due to their limited receptive fields, CNNs primarily focus on local connections,which hampers their ability to accurately reconstruct the complex capillary network.
elements.sent-2411-107: In contrast, transformer-based models, with their ability to capture long-range dependencies, generally reconstructed capillaries more effectively.
elements.sent-2411-108: Among all the tested methods, Freqformer achieved the most detailed reconstruction of capillaries with minimal blurring, outperforming both CNN-based and other transformer-based models.
elements.sent-2411-109: Notably, all methods successfully enhanced major blood vessels, as these structures are relatively simple and continuous.
elements.sent-2411-110: The average image metrics across the test set are presented in Table~\ref{table1 , with the best and second-best values highlighted in bold and underlined, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-111: Freqformer, achieved the best performance across all metrics.
elements.sent-2411-112: Compared to the second-best method (ART), Freqformer demonstrated a 0.32 dB improvement in PSNR and superior structural similarity, without increasing computational overhead in terms of giga multiply-accumulate operations per second (GMACs) or parameters (Params).
elements.sent-2411-113: While the CNN-based NAFNet performed competitively, even surpassing the transformer-based Restormer in certain metrics, its overall reconstruction capabilities remained inferior to the transformer models.
elements.sent-2411-114: As shown in Table~\ref{table1 , transformer-based models generally outperform CNN-based models, though often requiring significantly higher GMACs and Params.}
elements.sent-2411-115: Transformer-based models may have high space complexity, significantly increasing GPU memory usage.
elements.sent-2411-116: Among the transformer-based models, Freqformer required minimal GPU memory during training, half to that needed by Xformer or Restormer (Fig.~\ref{fig4 ).}
elements.sent-2411-117: This efficient memory usage highlights Freqformer’s advantage in balancing performance and space efficiency.

elements.sent-2411-118: The common strategy for reducing space complexity is shifted window (SW) attention~\cite{liu2021swin .}
elements.sent-2411-119: In order to reduce space complexity to a linear scale, SW attention operates by dividing the feature map into non-overlapping windows and applying conventional MHA within each window.
elements.sent-2411-120: To enable cross-window interaction, the windows are shifted by half their size.
elements.sent-2411-121: However, because the attention mechanism is applied locally within each window, SW attention may not fully capture long-range pixel interactions, even with window shifting.
elements.sent-2411-122: Consequently, SW attention may not fully leverage the power of the transformer architecture for global context representation.
elements.sent-2411-123: Here, we replaced the cross-covariance (CC) attention in Freqformer with SW attention (window size 10\(\times\)10).
elements.sent-2411-124: As shown in Figs.~\ref{fig5 (c) and (d), the capillaries generated by Freqformer with SW attention appeared more blurred compared to those generated by Freqformer with CC attention.}
elements.sent-2411-125: Additionally, Freqformer with SW attention failed to reconstruct certain capillaries, as indicated by the blue arrow in the figure.
elements.sent-2411-126: In contrast, Freqformer with CC attention achieved superior image metrics while requiring relatively lower peak GPU memory, as summarized in Table~\ref{table2 .}
elements.sent-2411-127: This demonstrates that CC attention achieves high performance in reconstruction while requiring less space complexity, making it a more effective choice for high-resolution retinal OCTA reconstruction.

elements.sent-2411-167: While the results demonstrate substantial improvements of Freqformer in both visual and quantitative performance compared to other methods, there are several limitations that may affect its broader applicability and impact.
elements.sent-2411-168: First, Freqformer’s reliance on high-definition ground truth derived from merged OCTA volumes presents a limitation, particularly in clinical settings where multiple scans may not be feasible due to patient comfort, time constraints, or technical limitations.
elements.sent-2411-169: This dependency on merged volumes means that Freqformer may not perform optimally on single-scan data without high-definition references, limiting its utility in certain real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-170: Second, despite our efforts to optimize space complexity, the computational demand of transformer network may limit the deployment of Freqformer in clinical environments lacking high-performance GPU hardware.
elements.sent-2411-171: Further research into streamlining the architecture or adapting it for less powerful devices would improve Freqformer's accessibility and ease of use.
elements.sent-2411-172: Third, our study involved only healthy subjects.
elements.sent-2411-173: Although we anticipate Freqformer can be applied directly or with minimal customization to pathological cases, the unique vascular patterns—such as capillary dropout or increased vessel tortuosity in diseased retinas—may challenge its generalizability across diverse patient populations, especially in scans with a large field of view.
elements.sent-2411-174: Future studies to evaluate and potentially adapt Freqformer for such pathologic cases will be necessary to confirm its broader applicability.
elements.sent-2411-175: In conclusion, this study presents Freqformer, a novel frequency-domain Transformer architecture designed to enhance 3-D visualization of retinal circulation in OCTA images.
elements.sent-2411-176: By integrating a cross-covariance attention mechanism and a complex-valued frequency-domain module, Freqformer effectively addresses limitations of conventional OCTA processing, delivering detailed, high-definition visualizations of retinal microvasculature with improved capillary continuity and reduced noise.
elements.sent-2411-177: Experimental results demonstrate that Freqformer outperforms state-of-the-art CNN-based and Transformer-based methods, offering superior enhancement and generalizability across different fields of view.
elements.sent-2411-178: The enhanced 3-D OCTA volumes enable an in-depth analysis of retinal vascular structures, revealing critical features such as capillary vortices and avascular zones, which are essential for understanding retinal health and disease.
elements.sent-2411-179: Despite limitations, including computational demand and reliance on high-definition ground truth during training, Freqformer shows significant promise as a powerful tool for retinal imaging.
elements.sent-2411-180: Future efforts will focus on optimizing Freqformer for clinical deployment, enhancing its efficiency, and expanding its capabilities for flow quantification and the analysis of diseased retinas.
elements.sent-2411-181: These advancements will further solidify Freqformer’s role in advancing the diagnosis and monitoring of retinal vascular diseases.. \bibliographystyle{IEEEtran.}
elements.sent-2411-182: Objective: To achieve accurate 3-D reconstruction and quantitative analysis of human retinal vasculature from a single optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) scan.
elements.sent-2411-183: Methods: We introduce Freqformer, a novel Transformer-based model featuring a dual-branch architecture that integrates a Transformer layer for capturing global spatial context with a complex-valued frequency-domain module designed for adaptive frequency enhancement.
elements.sent-2411-184: Freqformer was trained using single depth-plane OCTA images, utilizing volumetrically merged OCTA data as the ground truth.
elements.sent-2411-185: Performance was evaluated quantitatively through 2-D and 3-D image quality metrics.
elements.sent-2411-186: Furthermore, 3-D quantitative vascular metrics were conducted to quantify human retinal vasculature.
elements.sent-2411-187: Results: Freqformer substantially outperformed existing convolutional neural networks and Transformer-based methods, achieving superior image metrics.
elements.sent-2411-188: Importantly, the enhanced OCTA volumes show strong correlation with the merged volumes on vascular segment count, density, length, and flow index, further underscoring its reliability for quantitative vascular analysis.
elements.sent-2411-189: Additionally, Freqformer showed excellent generalization capability on larger field-of-view scans, surpassing the quality of conventional volumetric merging methods.
elements.sent-2411-190: Conclusion: Freqformer reliably generates high-definition 3-D retinal microvasculature from single-scan OCTA data, enabling precise vascular quantification comparable to standard volumetric merging methods.
elements.sent-2411-191: Significance: Freqformer has promising clinical applications in the early detection, detailed microvasculature analysis, and monitoring of retinal vascular diseases through advanced 3-D quantitative assessments.
elements.sent-2411-192: Recently, optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA) is emerging in diagnosing retinal diseases~\cite{hwang2015optical,taylor2024role .}
elements.sent-2411-193: However, the current analysis relies mainly on 2-D en face images projected from specific slabs, which may oversimplify and limit the complete elucidation of the complex 3-D organization of the retinal microvasculature.
elements.sent-2411-194: The technical challenge in performing direct 3-D analysis of OCTA volumes arises mainly from their limited signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), and therefore poor vascular connectivity.

elements.sent-2411-198: To the best of our knowledge, the only reported study on 3-D reconstruction of OCTA was performed by training the network on 2-D projection images and then applying it to segment each slice of 3-D OCTA volumes, which demonstrated improved blood vessel visibility~\cite{li2024visualization .}
elements.sent-2411-199: However, its limitations are obvious: a) Time-consuming manual annotation.
elements.sent-2411-200: Although 2-D projection–trained networks can be applied slice-by-slice for 3-D volumes, they often hallucinate vessels or miss faint capillaries when generalizing to slices, which is an artifact of deep learning’s inherent tendency to overfit the statistical priors of their training domain and falter under distribution shift.
elements.sent-2411-201: The resulting masks must therefore require extensive manual refinement, which is still labor-intensive and error-prone.
elements.sent-2411-202: Binarized segmentation methods identify vessel structures but discard flow contrast, which is essential for accurate vascular characterization~\cite{su2016calibration .}
elements.sent-2411-203: We introduce Freqformer as the first deep learning-based approach for 3-D enhancement of OCTA volume, leveraging Transformer-based architectures and frequency-domain learning to overcome the challenges of low SNR and poor vessel connectivity.
elements.sent-2411-204: This pioneering effort opens new avenues for high-resolution volumetric vascular imaging in both clinical and research settings, benefiting the downstream vascular quantification.
elements.sent-2411-205: The key contributions are as follows: (1) Introduction of Complex-Valued Frequency-Domain Module (CFDM).
elements.sent-2411-206: We propose CFDM, an innovative frequency-domain processing module that selectively enhances critical frequency components, especially mid- and high-frequency components which Transformer overlooks.
elements.sent-2411-207: CFDM uses complex-valued convolutions to compensate for the low-pass filtering property of multi-head attention in Transformer layers and adaptively boost whichever frequencies are most beneficial for feature maps at each stage of the network, ensuring a comprehensive representation of retinal microvasculature from both spatial and frequency domains.
elements.sent-2411-208: This module significantly improves capillary continuity and vessel integrity in depth-plane images.
elements.sent-2411-209: Freqformer enables direct volumetric reconstruction of retinal vasculature from a single scan, achieving high-fidelity 3-D OCTA enhancement with high consistency with merged volumes.
elements.sent-2411-210: By preserving full 3-D vascular integrity, Freqformer enables quantitative analysis of capillary segments, including vessel count, density, length and flow index.

elements.sent-2411-221: To accurately capture true retinal vasculature in 3-D, removing tail artifacts of blood vessels is required~\cite{hormel2021artifacts .}
elements.sent-2411-222: The tail artifacts are elongated axial streaks that appear immediately posterior to the true blood vessels (as denoted by the blue arrows in Fig.~\ref{fig1 ), obscuring true vascular structures and leading to inaccurate interpretation of vessel morphology or density.}

elements.sent-2411-225: To address this challenge, we propose a moving average subtraction filter (MASF) method: \begin{equation}\begin{aligned} \text{MASF}(A_{k}) = \sqrt{\mathrm{max}(A_{k}^{2} - \gamma\frac{1}{w} \sum_{i=k-w}^{k-1} A_{i}^{2}, 0)} \end{aligned} \label{eq1}\end{equation} where \(A_{k}\) denotes the value of the \(k\)-th pixel in an A-line, and \(\gamma\) and \(w\) represent the weight factor and window size, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-226: In this work, \(\gamma\) and \(w\) are empirically set to 0.8 and 11, respectively, as this configuration effectively suppresses tail artifacts while preserving capillary structures.
elements.sent-2411-227: This method leverages the fact that artifact voxels typically have smaller angiogram values than the real vessel voxels located anteriorly due to their weaker de-correlation between the repeated scans, a widely recognized characteristic in OCTA imaging~\cite{zhang2016projection, choi2020mean .}
elements.sent-2411-228: As a result, tail artifacts are effectively reduced, while the capillaries previously buried in the artifacts stand out.

elements.sent-2411-230: The result demonstrates that our method avoids both over- and under-suppression of tail artifacts, achieving high fidelity to the true vascular anatomy.

elements.sent-2411-300: As shown in Table~\ref{table2 , the integration of CFDM notably enhances reconstruction performance across all metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-301: While the Transformer layer alone also provides meaningful improvements by effectively capturing global spatial interactions, the introduction of CFDM further amplified these gains, highlighting CFDM's substantial and significant role in the network.
elements.sent-2411-302: The synergistic contributions of Transformer layer and CFDM ensure a more balanced and comprehensive feature representation, leveraging the complementary strengths of global spatial and frequency domain information.
elements.figure-2411-017: \centerline{\includegraphics[width=0.95\columnwidth]{F4.png}} \caption{Log-magnitude spectrum difference maps for the CFDM at (a) the first stage and (b) the second stage, as well as for the Transformer layer at (c) first stage and (d) second stage from the first channel of a representative block.} \label{fig4}
elements.sent-2411-303: To further illustrate CFDM's selective frequency-domain enhancement, its functionality is analyzed through log-magnitude spectrum difference maps, which quantify the changes in frequency components induced by CFDM~(Fig.~\ref{fig4 ).}
elements.sent-2411-304: Specifically, these difference maps are computed by subtracting the log-magnitude spectrum of the feature maps before and after CFDM processing for each channel individually.
elements.sent-2411-305: This approach allows us to visualize how CFDM selectively enhances certain frequency components.
elements.sent-2411-306: In these maps, a strong change (red regions) in specific frequency ranges, such as mid- or high-frequency components, indicates that CFDM actively amplifies those frequency features.
elements.sent-2411-307: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig4 , CFDM exhibits distinct frequency-dependent enhancement at different network stages, with unique amplification patterns observed at each stage.}
elements.sent-2411-308: This confirms that CFDM dynamically adapts its enhancement strategy based on feature representation needs.
elements.sent-2411-309: In contrast, the Transformer layer does not demonstrate this frequency-specific enhancement, as it primarily extracts spatial features.
elements.sent-2411-310: This distinction highlights the complementary roles of the CFDM and Transformer layer—where the Transformer layer preserves global spatial continuity while CFDM refines vascular structures by selectively enhancing critical frequency components.
elements.sent-2411-311: Furthermore, to validate the effectiveness of our current dual-branch architecture in leveraging the complementary strengths of the Transformer layer and CFDM, three alternative structures are compared with our design (Fig.~\ref{fig5 ).}
elements.sent-2411-312: Specifically, model (b) works by cascading the Transformer layer and the CFDM.
elements.sent-2411-313: Model (c) and (d) incorporate two common dual branch feature fusion mechanisms: (c) the channel fusion and (d) feature addition and channel fusion.
elements.sent-2411-314: As presented in Table.~\ref{table3 , the results demonstrate that our design (dual-branch architecture without feature fusion) outperforms other variants across all the metrics.}
elements.sent-2411-315: This superiority underscores the critical role of block architecture in effectively integrating spatial and frequency representations, demonstrating that the interference between spatial and frequency features can degrade performance or even negatively impact overall reconstruction quality.

elements.subsection-2411-015: 3-D Visualization and Quantification of Retinal Microvasculature
elements.sent-2411-316: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig6 , Freqformer enhanced OCTA volume from single scan achieves detailed 3-D visualization of retinal microvasculature.}
elements.sent-2411-317: Specifically, avascular zones are clearly identified adjacent to arteries (indicated by white arrows), but absent near veins (Fig.~\ref{fig6 (a)).}
elements.sent-2411-318: Capillary vortices, which are a spider-like arrangement of capillaries in the deep capillary plexus (DCP), are resolved with their connection confidently traced to retinal venules (Fig.~\ref{fig6 (b)).}
elements.sent-2411-319: To quantify the retinal microvasculature in 3-D, capillary segment count, segment density, segment length, and flow index within inner retinal volume are calculated and analyzed.
elements.sent-2411-320: Both the enhanced (E) OCTA volumes and merged (M) OCTA volumes resolve more capillary segments (\(\delta\) (M-S)/M: 20\%, and \(\delta\) (E-S)/M: 22\%) than the original single-scan (S) OCTA (Fig.~\ref{fig6 (c)).}
elements.sent-2411-321: Additionally, consistent with merged volumes, enhanced OCTA volumes demonstrate a higher percentage of longer capillary segments, validating the improved continuity of the vascular network.
elements.sent-2411-322: Compared to the single-scan volumes, the enhanced volumes exhibit an increased flow index (average signal value within 3-D blood vessel masks), approaching ideal levels to the merged volumes (binarized vessel mask obtained from merged volumes and consistent across all three volumes).
elements.sent-2411-323: Furthermore, NRMSE scores between enhanced and merged volumes are calculated for all 3-D vascular quantification metrics to evaluate the quantification error, with Freqformer achieving the lowest NRMSE across all methods (Table~\ref{table4 ).}
elements.sent-2411-324: Concretely, relative to the strongest Transformer baseline (Xformer), Freqformer reduces NRMSE by $\sim$7\% for segment count, $\sim$10\% for segment density, $\sim$4\% for segment length, and $\sim$33\% for flow index; versus the best CNN (NAFNet), the reductions are $\sim$32\%, $\sim$37\%, $\sim$31\%, and $\sim$35\%, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-325: Notably, Freqformer’s largest margin is on the flow index, indicating that the benefits extend beyond morphological continuity to a more faithful recovery of flow-related OCTA signal intensities.
elements.sent-2411-326: Taken together, these results have direct practical implications.
elements.sent-2411-327: Achieving near-merged-volume fidelity from a single acquisition removes the need for multi-volume merging—rarely feasible in routine clinics, especially for large-FOV protocols—thereby shortening examination time, reducing motion/registration failures, and lowering patient burden (children, elderly, poor fixation).
elements.sent-2411-328: Moreover, the improved capillary continuity and recovered OCTA signal stabilize downstream 3-D vascular quantification, which improves repeatability; in longitudinal or treatment-response settings this translates into a smaller minimal detectable change and greater statistical power for the same cohort size, enabling finer tracking of disease trajectories and therapy effects.

elements.sent-2411-329: These en face images offer higher SNR than single depth-plane images, making them well-suited for visualizing retinal microvasculature and training deep learning models for enhancement~\cite{gao2020reconstruction, kadomoto2020enhanced .}
elements.sent-2411-330: Unfortunately, the depth information is collapsed in these 2-D images, thereby hindering the appreciation and quantification of 3-D retinal vascular network.
elements.sent-2411-331: Here, with Freqformer, we revealed the organization of retinal arteries and veins and extracted capillary segments in 3-D (Fig.~\ref{fig6 ).}
elements.sent-2411-332: Using these data provided with commercial OCT, we successfully quantified the capillary segment density in vivo in 3-D for human retinas for the first time.
elements.sent-2411-333: In the future, we hope that more detailed quantification of 3-D microvasculature would provide us more insights into the blood flow and retinal tissue oxygenation\cite{corliss2019methods .}
elements.sent-2411-334: In this study, we opted for 2-D single-depth-plane angiogram images rather than 3-D patches for training and reconstruction due to both computational constraints and feature extraction limitations.
elements.sent-2411-335: First, 3-D patch approaches introduce substantial computational overhead, making them impractical for real-time clinical applications.
elements.sent-2411-336: OCTA volumes are inherently high resolution, and applying 3-D convolutions or MHA across patches would exponentially increase GPU memory requirements and inference time, making it infeasible for clinical deployment.
elements.sent-2411-337: Second, while 3-D convolutions can theoretically enhance spatial continuity, they assume uniform information density across all dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-338: However, in OCTA, the en-face (lateral) images contain much richer vascular contrast than cross-sectional B-scans (axial direction).
elements.sent-2411-339: If 3-D patches were used, high-contrast vascular features from en-face images could be diluted by low-contrast axial features, making it inefficient for extracting capillary details \cite{huang2024upping .}
elements.sent-2411-340: To assess the relative effectiveness of 2-D versus 3-D networks, we implemented 3-D counterparts of Freqformer as well as other networks in Table~\ref{table1 .}
elements.sent-2411-341: To be specific, all 2-D convolutional layers, pixel shuffle, layer normalization, pooling operations and custom modules (e.g., FFT or masking strategy) were replaced with their 3-D counterparts while preserving the original configurations.
elements.sent-2411-342: In addition, MHA and MLP in Transformer layers were adapted to process volumetric tokens without altering the original formulation.
elements.sent-2411-343: Following common practice for 3-D networks, we adopted a patch-based training and inference scheme to accommodate GPU memory limits.
elements.sent-2411-344: We used the same 20 volumetric scans as in the 2-D experiments for training and validation.
elements.sent-2411-345: We first localized the retinal depth range in each volume using our OCT B-scan segmentation algorithm, and then extracted patches exclusively from this retinal region to avoid excessive background in OCTA.
elements.sent-2411-346: Each OCTA retinal volume was partitioned into non-overlapping 3-D patches of size 32\(\times\)64\(\times\)64 (Depth\(\times\)Height\(\times\)Width) and split 9:1 for training and validation.
elements.sent-2411-347: For testing, we used the same 24 scans as in the 2-D experiments.
elements.sent-2411-348: During inference, we performed dense sliding-window evaluation with 32\(\times\)64\(\times\)64 patches and strides of 16\(\times\)28\(\times\)28, where overlapping voxel predictions were averaged to mitigate boundary artifacts and ensure spatial consistency.
elements.sent-2411-349: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig7 , converting the networks to 3-D does not change the coarse performance hierarchy and Freqformer achieves best performance in both 2-D and 3-D, indicating that its advantage is not tied to the dimensionality of the backbone.}
elements.sent-2411-350: The extent of the performance change associated with the transition from 2-D to 3-D varies across model types.
elements.sent-2411-351: For CNN baselines, the change is modest, with only small deviations between the 2-D and 3-D versions.
elements.sent-2411-352: In contrast, Transformer-based models exhibit more pronounced variation when moved to 3-D.
elements.sent-2411-353: This is likely because flattening 3-D patches into token sequences in MHA breaks the native volumetric geometry, making it harder for Transformer-based networks to model geometric relationships even with relative positional embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-354: In addition, we selected the top CNN baseline (NAFNet) and the strongest Transformer baseline (Xformer) excluding Freqformer, together with their 3-D counterparts, for head-to-head comparisons on inference time, image quality and NRMSE of 3-D vascular quantification.
elements.sent-2411-355: As shown in Table~\ref{table5 , the 3-D variants do not improve image metrics or quantification error and instead incur nearly 10\(\times\) longer inference time than their original version, which is partly attributable to overlapping sliding-window strategy.}
elements.sent-2411-356: These results support our design choice to enhance volumes slice by slice with Freqformer, which offers the best balance of image quality, 3-D vascular quantification accuracy, runtime, and GPU requirement.

elements.sent-2411-357: To further evaluate the generalization performance of Freqformer, we applied it to OCTA images acquired using the same device but with an expanded field of view (FOV, 6\(\times\)6 \(\text m \text m^{\text 2}\)).
elements.sent-2411-358: Large FOV scans are increasingly used in clinical settings to capture a broader range of pathological features, such as non-perfusion areas, microaneurysms, and neovascularization ~\cite{zhang2016wide .}
elements.sent-2411-359: However, with a fixed A-line scanning rate and the same or slightly increased A-line number, expanding the FOV results in lower resolution, leading to decreased image quality of retinal microvasculature.
elements.sent-2411-360: While volume merging can partially compensate for the reduced quality, the improvement is often limited, and precise registration is further complicated by scan-to-scan displacements due to fixation shifts.
elements.sent-2411-361: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig8 , merging 10 volumes from 6\(\times\)6 \(\text m \text m^{\text 2}\) scans partially improves vascular connectivity and signal-to-noise ratio (SNR)~\cite{wang2022compressive , defined as the ratio of the mean signal within vascular regions to the standard deviation of noise in the foveal avascular zone, compared to a single scan.}}
elements.sent-2411-362: However, the resulting image still shows a lower SNR than the standard 3\(\times\)3 \(\text m \text m^{\text 2}\) merged image.
elements.sent-2411-363: In contrast, Freqformer reconstructs capillary structures with high morphological consistency to the merged reference while also achieving higher SNR values (\(\Delta\)=1.98 ± 0.59 (SD), p$<$0.001, N=10).
elements.sent-2411-364: These findings demonstrate Freqformer’s capability to perform super resolution imaging for large FOV scans (such as 12\(\times\)12 \(\text m \text m^{\text 2}\)), offering a viable alternative to conventional volume-merging workflows.
elements.sent-2411-365: Its robustness and versatility make it a promising tool for broader clinical applications.
elements.sent-2411-366: First, Our study used PLEX® Elite 9000 SS-OCT for OCTA acquisition.
elements.sent-2411-367: While we demonstrated generalization across different scanning densities and fields of view, the model’s performance on other OCTA devices (e.g., SD-OCT or different commercial systems) remains unverified.
elements.sent-2411-368: Further validation across diverse imaging modalities and hardware platforms is needed.
elements.sent-2411-369: Second, this study primarily focused on healthy subjects, which may limit the model’s generalizability to pathological cases with vascular abnormalities such as capillary dropout, neovascularization, and vessel tortuosity.
elements.sent-2411-370: Evaluating Freqformer on diseased retinas will be necessary to assess its robustness in real-world clinical applications.
elements.sent-2411-371: Third, while Freqformer effectively reconstructs depth-resolved vasculature, direct quantitative validation of full 3-D vascular topology remains challenging due to the lack of a well-established ground truth for volumetric vascular structures in OCTA.
elements.sent-2411-372: Future work should integrate automated vessel segmentation and topology analysis to improve 3-D vasculature validation.
elements.sent-2411-373: Finally, despite our efforts to optimize space complexity, the computational demand of Freqformer is still higher than that of CNNs.
elements.sent-2411-374: Further research into streamlining the architecture for less powerful GPU would improve Freqformer's accessibility and ease of use.
elements.sent-2411-375: In conclusion, this study presents Freqformer, a novel frequency-domain Transformer architecture designed to enhance OCTA volumes and thus the entire retinal vasculature in 3-D.
elements.sent-2411-376: By integrating complex-valued frequency-domain module and cross-covariance attention mechanism, Freqformer effectively addresses limitations of conventional OCTA processing, delivering detailed, high-definition visualization of retinal microvasculature with improved capillary continuity and reduced noise.
elements.sent-2411-377: Experimental results demonstrate that Freqformer outperforms state-of-the-art CNN-based and Transformer-based methods, offering superior enhancement and enabling capillary segment-wise quantification in human retinas.
elements.sent-2411-378: Additionally, Freqformer displays great generalizability on the 6\(\times\)6 \(\text m \text m^{\text 2}\) protocol.
elements.sent-2411-379: Despite limitations, like the lack of training on pathological data and requirement of high-definition ground truth during training, Freqformer shows significant promise as a powerful tool for retinal imaging.
elements.sent-2411-380: Future efforts will focus on optimizing Freqformer for clinical deployment, enhancing its efficiency, and expanding its capabilities for flow quantification and analysis of diseased retinas.
